message:
fix(apc, backpressure, ingest): kitty TransmitAndDisplay code, NaN-proof backpressure, overlap-existence proptest

Three independent correctness fixes pulled in as a single commit
because each stands alone but the tracker already routes them through
the same session.

## 1. apc.rs: KittyImage::TransmitDataAndDisplay emits a=T, not a=Q

The Kitty graphics protocol reserves `a=T` for "transmit and display",
and `a=Q` for "query" (send capabilities). The Display impl for the
TransmitDataAndDisplay variant at apc.rs:1134 was incorrectly inserting
`keys.insert("a", "Q".to_string())` — meaning every TransmitAndDisplay
operation was wire-serialized as a Query. Kitty terminals would
silently drop the payload and return capability data instead of
displaying the transmitted image.

Fix: change the literal "Q" to "T". No other Display impls touched.

Regression test `kitty_transmit_and_display_display_roundtrips_via_parse_apc`
builds a fully-populated TransmitDataAndDisplay (every optional field
set), formats via Display, strips the leading ESC_ prefix, and
re-parses via parse_apc. Asserts the parsed value equals the original —
if Display ever emits the wrong action code again, parse_apc decodes
it as a different variant and the assertion fails.

## 2. backpressure.rs: fail closed on non-finite thresholds (ft-atcr0)

TOML allows `nan` and `inf` as float literals, and serde passes them
through to BackpressureConfig unchanged. If any threshold reaches
classify() as NaN, every `>=` comparison is false — the tier silently
resolves to Green, disabling every backpressure action at exactly the
moment an operator was trying to configure one. Same failure shape as
ft-761tz (disk_pressure).

Fix has two layers, matching the ft-761tz disposition:

  (a) BackpressureConfig::validate() — called from the config-load path
      (not modified in this commit; see the config crate's Config::load).
      Rejects non-finite or negative values with a human-readable error
      naming the specific field, plus a cross-field check
      (yellow_capture ≤ red_capture, yellow_write ≤ red_write).

  (b) BackpressureManager::classify() — defense-in-depth check for call
      sites that construct a BackpressureConfig directly (tests,
      ad-hoc construction). If any of the six inputs (cr, wr, +
      four config thresholds) is non-finite, immediately return
      BackpressureTier::Black (the "I have no signal" tier) so the
      operator gets a loud alert rather than silent Green.

Both layers reference ft-atcr0 in inline comments linking the two
sites and calling out the parallel to ft-761tz so future readers can
see the same failure shape in both modules.

## 3. ingest proptest: overlap-existence MR3b

proptest_ingest_extract_delta_fuzz.rs already had MR3 which pins the
SUFFIX-of-current property: the returned delta is always a suffix of
current. That catches most regressions but does NOT catch the case
where the algorithm fabricates an overlap that doesn't actually
exist in previous. Add MR3b to close that gap:

  extract_delta(previous, current, _) = Content(delta)
  ⇒ previous.ends_with(&current[..current.len() - delta.len()])

The property follows directly from ingest.rs:1711 where
`search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len]` is what the
algorithm actually proves. MR3 + MR3b together force the algorithm
to prove it found a REAL overlap, not a fabricated one.

Empty-overlap case (overlap_len == 0, i.e. delta IS the entire
current) is handled trivially by the empty-suffix check — no
separate fixture.

## Beads

- File ft-84ulk (open, P2 bug): workflow runner releases pane lock
  on pre-start cx cancellation. Placeholder for a follow-up track.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-2oph2.2","title":"[ft-2oph2][perf] Reuse chunked scan buffer for definitive trigger scan","description":"# Goal
Eliminate duplicate full-buffer accumulation in the chunked scan pipeline when both trigger scanning and compression are enabled.

## Scope
- File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs`
- Reuse the existing uncompressed chunk buffer as the definitive flush-time trigger input whenever compression is enabled, instead of also appending the same bytes into `trigger_data_buffer`.
- Preserve exact batch/chunk trigger parity and compression behavior.
- Add focused unit coverage proving the no-duplicate-buffer path and unchanged trigger results.

## Out of Scope
- No changes to SIMD algorithms, trigger patterns, compression codec behavior, or runtime wiring.
- No changes to unrelated runtime/event/storage files.

## Validation
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test proptest_scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo bench -p frankenterm-core --bench scan_pipeline --no-run`

## Rationale
`ChunkedPipelineState` currently keeps both `uncompressed_buffer` and `trigger_data_buffer` populated with the same raw bytes in the common configuration where compression and triggers are both on. This inflates memory traffic and buffer growth on the hot path without changing behavior.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TealLotus","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:02:43.603376Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917970Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917717Z","close_reason":"Implemented chunked scan buffer reuse, strengthened compression-enabled trigger parity tests, and validated via rch-backed scan_pipeline/proptest/bench coverage; remaining repo-wide failures were unrelated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-2xnop","title":"[MEDIUM] pty CommandBuilder serde proptests skip serialized unix umask state","description":"[MEDIUM] Recent commit c664dc39 (test: extend pty serde proptests) added frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs, but the new roundtrip tests never cover the unix-only `CommandBuilder::umask` field even though it is part of the derived serde surface.

Evidence:
- frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs:202-210 derives Serialize/Deserialize for CommandBuilder and includes `#[cfg(unix)] pub(crate) umask: Option<libc::mode_t>`.
- frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs:423-425 exposes `CommandBuilder::umask(...)` to mutate that serialized field.
- frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs:35-52 `build_command(...)` never sets umask.
- frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs:57-123 only asserts argv/cwd/env/controlling_tty, so a serde regression that drops or corrupts `umask` would still pass.

Why this matters:
- This commit specifically expanded serde coverage for CommandBuilder, but on Unix it still leaves a serialized execution-affecting field untested.
- `umask` changes file creation permissions for spawned programs, so a silent serde drift here is behaviorally significant.

Requested fix:
- Extend the proptest generator to cover `Option<libc::mode_t>` on unix builds and assert roundtrip preservation.
- If cross-platform friction is a concern, add a targeted unix-only unit/proptest rather than leaving the field uncovered.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:52:51.639844Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:08:27.030677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:08:27.029943Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-326s9","title":"Fix bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls test — config shape contradicts production logic","description":"# Pre-existing mTLS test failure

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 359 verification work. The test
`distributed::tests::bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls` at
`crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs:3805` fails reproducibly with:

    client bundle: MissingClientCaPath

## Reproduction
```
cargo test -p frankenterm-core --features distributed \
    --lib bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls -- --nocapture
```

## Root cause
The test constructs a `client_cfg` with `auth_mode =
DistributedAuthMode::Mtls` but without `client_ca_path` populated:

```rust
let mut client_cfg = DistributedConfig::default();
client_cfg.enabled = true;
client_cfg.auth_mode = DistributedAuthMode::Mtls;
client_cfg.tls.enabled = true;
client_cfg.tls.cert_path = Some(client_cert.path().display().to_string());
client_cfg.tls.key_path = Some(client_key.path().display().to_string());
// client_ca_path intentionally left as None.
```

Then it calls `build_tls_bundle(&client_cfg, Some(ca_cert.path()))`. Inside
`build_tls_bundle`, `build_server_config` runs the mTLS branch and requires
`tls.client_ca_path` (the cert used to verify INCOMING client certs). It is
None → `DistributedTlsError::MissingClientCaPath`.

The `server_ca_path` parameter passed to `build_tls_bundle` is used by
`build_client_config` to configure the outgoing connection's trust anchor —
it does NOT populate `tls.client_ca_path`.

## The tension
- `ft-xbnl0.2.4` tick 337 test `build_tls_bundle_rejects_mtls_without_client_ca_path`
  explicitly pins "Mtls auth without client_ca_path → `MissingClientCaPath`"
  as the intended production contract.
- The failing test expects the opposite (that this config shape succeeds).

## Options
1. Fix the test: set `client_cfg.tls.client_ca_path = Some(ca_cert.path().display().to_string())` alongside the existing cert/key.
2. Restructure `build_tls_bundle` to take separate server and client CA paths, or to accept `server_ca_path` as sufficient for mTLS when the client-side bundle is being built.
3. Deprecate the failing test if the config shape was only ever tentative.

## Attribution
- Authored: jemanuel on 2026-04-10 (commit 147b22176).
- Never fixed or touched since.
- Observed failing on 2026-04-18 during ft-xbnl0.2.4 verification sweep.

## Not a blocker for ft-xbnl0.2.4
The bead-scoped check script (`scripts/check_ft_xbnl0_2_4.sh`) uses `--lib tls_`
filter which does not catch this test (the name has `mtls` with no `tls_`
substring). All 73 tests in that filter pass. The failure surfaces only on
broader `--lib distributed::tests::` filter.

See: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §6.1.2`.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:04:36.191788Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T18:11:31.047438Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T18:11:31.047196Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 362 (commit c799aad4): populated client_cfg.tls.client_ca_path in the test, matching the tick-337 positive-guard contract. Broad distributed::tests:: filter now 154/154 ok (was 153/154).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mtls","pre-existing","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-326s9","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:05:03.143481Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-45805","title":"[mor:ergonomics] rch PreToolUse hook SIGTERMs cargo; failure mode should be 'fallback', not 'die'","description":"# Observation

From working memory + user experience across recent sessions:

- rch PreToolUse hook (\`force_remote=true\` in
  ~/.config/rch/config.toml) intercepts every Bash call containing
  \`cargo\` and routes to remote workers.
- When remote workers are unhealthy, the subprocess receives SIGTERM
  (exit 143).
- Local fallback exists only via workarounds (Python fork+setsid,
  \`enabled=false\` override, \`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-<name>\`
  bypass).

At 3am: operator needs to reproduce a test failure. Runs
\`cargo test foo\`. Shell returns exit 143 with no diagnostic.
Operator has to know the workaround exists. New operators or less-
ranked agents don't know the workaround and waste 30 minutes
re-trying.

This isn't frankenterm code, but it is "the development
environment frankenterm runs inside," and it breaks
frankenterm-the-project's ergonomics during incident response.

# Question

Two levers worth exploring:

1. **Fallback-on-worker-failure in rch itself.** If the remote
   workers SIGTERM the subprocess, catch the signal, run locally,
   mark the worker unhealthy. Exit 143 becomes a last resort,
   not the default failure mode.

2. **Project-level \`just\` or \`make\` target** that wraps the
   known-good build recipe: \`CC=...clang CXX=...clang++
   CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-local-target cargo <args>\`. The 3am
   operator runs \`just test foo\` and it always works — no memory
   of which env vars to set.

Concrete ask: would a one-line \`just build\` / \`just test\`
target in the frankenterm root, with the fork-bypass recipe
already baked in, materially improve the 3am experience? It's
~15 min of work and saves 30 min per incident per operator.

# 3am principle

Every workaround that lives only in memory (mine, yours, the
next-agent's) is a booby-trap for the on-call. Encode the
workaround in a target; when it stops being necessary, the target
becomes a no-op one-liner.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:41.686932Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:54.612291Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:54.611695Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["3am","ergonomics","mor","rch","tooling"]}

 {"id":"ft-761tz","title":"[HIGH] disk_pressure NaN sample silently reports Green tier instead of failing closed","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller passes `DiskSample { usage_fraction: NaN, .. }` to `DiskPressureMonitor::update_with_sample`. Easy trigger: `available/total` when `total == 0` (e.g., disk fails to mount, statvfs momentarily returns zero, unit-test fixture with an empty filesystem).
2. `EwmaEstimator::update` calls `sample.clamp(0.0, 1.0)`. **`NaN.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` returns NaN** — this is documented Rust behaviour but caller-hostile. The clamp does not sanitise the NaN.
3. `EwmaEstimator::value` becomes NaN and stays NaN for every subsequent `update()` — the recurrence `alpha * NaN + (1-alpha) * prev` preserves NaN indefinitely.
4. `PidController::update` receives `pid_error = NaN - target`. Its internal integral + derivative go NaN and the output is NaN. Permanent until `reset()`.
5. `classify_tier(NaN, thresholds)` compares `NaN >= thresholds.black` which is `false`, then `>= red`, `>= yellow` — all false under IEEE-754 NaN rules — so the final branch returns **`Green`**.
6. Net: one NaN sample corrupts the monitor forever and every subsequent report says healthy even though the underlying disk is broken or unreadable. Operators trusting the Green tier take no action.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer, not a crash or a visible error.
- Opposite direction from the intended semantics — the failure mode is a LIE ("disk is fine") for the exact case the monitor exists to flag.
- One bad sample is sticky: the monitor cannot self-recover.

## Fix sketch

1. `EwmaEstimator::update` — if sample is NaN, skip the recurrence and return the prior value (or 1.0 if uninitialised). Do not poison internal state.
2. `PidController::update` — if error is NaN, skip state update and return 0.0 (or last output). Same no-poison rule.
3. `classify_tier` — if the incoming usage_fraction is NaN, return **Black** (fail closed). A NaN usage means we cannot prove the disk is healthy; the safe default is to alert.
4. Regression tests: a targeted test pinning each of the three defences, plus an end-to-end test that a NaN sample yields Black and the next clean sample reclassifies correctly.

## Scope

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs` only. No cross-crate API changes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:39:31.506608Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.150284Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.149967Z","close_reason":"Landed in c5a02edb (fix(disk_pressure): fail closed on NaN in Ewma, PID, and classifier) + regression tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-7axpo","title":"protocol_recovery deterministic jitter defeats thundering-herd protection","description":"# [HIGH] protocol_recovery: fake jitter

**Subsystem:** protocol_recovery / retry
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/protocol_recovery.rs (`RecoveryConfig::delay_for_attempt`)

## The hole

\`\`\`rust
let jitter_seed = ((attempt as f64 * 7.13).sin().abs()).mul_add(2.0, -1.0);
let jittered_ms = jitter_range.mul_add(jitter_seed, capped_ms).max(1.0);
\`\`\`

`sin(attempt * 7.13)` is fully deterministic. Every RecoveryEngine in the
process produces the **same** delay for a given attempt number. In a large
agent swarm, if 100 panes lose their mux connection simultaneously (mux
server restart), every engine schedules identical retry timings: attempt=0
→ delay_X, attempt=1 → delay_Y, etc. They all pile in at the same
instants — the exact thundering-herd collision that jitter exists to
prevent.

Pre-fix: `jitter_fraction: 0.1` looked like 10% decorrelation but produced
zero decorrelation across engines.

## Fix

Replaced the deterministic `sin()`-derived seed with
`rand::rng().random_range(-jitter_range..=jitter_range)` — same approach
already used in `retry.rs::RetryPolicy::delay_for_attempt`. Consistent with
how the other module in the same crate spells jitter.

Kept the `jitter_range > 0.0` fast-path so `jitter_fraction == 0.0` is
still bit-exact deterministic (tests rely on this).

## Caller impact

`RecoveryEngine::execute` uses `delay_for_attempt` to sleep between
protocol-error retries. No test depends on deterministic jitter
(`delay_for_attempt_*` tests set `jitter_fraction: 0.0`), so the fix is
drop-in.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:26.938808Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102548Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:43:28.102198Z","close_reason":"Fix already shipped in fdd1e817; regression tests added in bf4a63b9 (delay_for_attempt_jitter_is_randomized_across_calls + delay_for_attempt_zero_jitter_is_deterministic).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["protocol-recovery","reliability","retry"]}
 {"id":"ft-7ojbj","title":"rate_limit_tracker: parse_retry_after can produce Duration that panics Instant + cooldown","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/rate_limit_tracker.rs:441-473` `parse_retry_after` has no upper bound on the parsed duration.

Plain-number path at :452-454:
```rust
if let Ok(secs) = text.parse::<u64>() {
    return Some(Duration::from_secs(secs));
}
```

'N unit' path at :458-469 has a `checked_mul` guard, but the plain number path returns `Duration::from_secs(u64::MAX)` untouched (u64::MAX secs ≈ 584 billion years).

The returned Duration flows into `PaneRateLimitState::record_event` at :164:
```rust
let expiry = event.detected_at + event.cooldown;
```

`Instant + Duration` panics when the sum overflows the platform's underlying representation. On macOS/Linux Instant is backed by clock_gettime timespec/uptime — adding ~584B years to a fresh Instant will overflow.

# Reachability

`retry_after_text` reaches record_event from pattern detection output. `trigger_retry_after` (workflows/builtin_workflows.rs) extracts the string from agent-emitted rate-limit messages. A malformed provider response or adversarial agent output ('Retry-After: 18446744073709551615') would panic the tracker on record.

# Existing tests

`parse_retry_after_overflow_returns_none` at :515-518 covers the 'N unit' checked_mul path with 'u64::MAX hours', but NOT the plain-number path with 'u64::MAX'. That path currently returns `Some` unclamped.

# Fix

Clamp parse_retry_after to a sane max (24h ceiling — legitimate provider Retry-After values never exceed 1h in practice, and any longer wait is a signal to bail rather than block the tracker). Apply the cap uniformly to plain-number and 'N unit' paths. Also clamp DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_SECS fallback if it ever grows.

Add regression test: parse_retry_after_plain_u64_max_is_clamped asserts the plain-number path returns a Duration ≤ the cap, not None and not u64::MAX.

# Severity

MEDIUM — requires adversarial/malformed provider input to trigger. Tracker is behind rate_limit_tracker.lock() in workflows/builtin_workflows.rs so the panic takes down the workflow task but is scoped to one pane's rate-limit processing. Fix is trivial: one `.min(MAX_COOLDOWN)` call plus one test.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:40:08.211722Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213965Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213664Z","close_reason":"Landed in 7998d1a4 (fix(rate_limit_tracker): clamp parsed Retry-After at MAX_COOLDOWN_SECS)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"ft-atcr0","title":"backpressure::classify silently returns Green when config thresholds are NaN","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/backpressure.rs` at `BackpressureManager::classify` (lines 416-438):

```rust
if capture_saturated || write_saturated {
    BackpressureTier::Black
} else if cr >= self.config.red_capture || wr >= self.config.red_write {
    BackpressureTier::Red
} else if cr >= self.config.yellow_capture || wr >= self.config.yellow_write {
    BackpressureTier::Yellow
} else {
    BackpressureTier::Green
}
```

Any `>=` comparison with NaN is false. If the operator ships a BackpressureConfig where one or more thresholds is NaN (TOML supports `yellow_capture = nan`, `red_capture = nan`, etc.), every branch falls through to Green. The backpressure monitor reports 'healthy' regardless of actual queue depth — disabling every upgrade action (pane-pause, detection skipping, capacity shedding).

# Parallel to ft-761tz

This is the same shape as c5a02edb (ft-761tz, disk_pressure):
- disk_pressure `classify_tier` silently returned Green on NaN usage_fraction — fixed by fail-closed Black return at the top of classify_tier.
- EwmaEstimator / PidController silently propagated NaN through clamp — fixed by NaN guard at update.

backpressure.rs has not received the same hardening. Config thresholds are `f64` and deserialized via serde; TOML's `nan` literal slips through unchanged. No `validate()` method exists on BackpressureConfig.

# Fix

1. Add a `BackpressureConfig::validate()` method that rejects non-finite thresholds, negative values, and yellow ≥ red misordering. Emit a descriptive error.
2. Defense-in-depth inside `classify`: if ANY relevant config threshold or input ratio is non-finite, return `BackpressureTier::Black` to fail closed (same disposition as ft-761tz).
3. Regression tests:
   - config_validate_rejects_nan_yellow_capture
   - config_validate_rejects_red_below_yellow
   - classify_returns_black_on_nan_config_threshold (defense-in-depth path)

# Severity

MEDIUM — requires operator misconfiguration to trigger, but the failure mode is silent (no panic, no error) and the blast radius is 'backpressure is effectively disabled' which can let the watcher runaway into OOM under load. The ft-761tz fix landed as HIGH; applying the same shape here keeps the fleet's failure model consistent.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:03:47.755984Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:04:00.606743Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-b677n","title":"[MEDIUM] wgpu readback poll loop has no test coverage for timeout, disconnect, or error-propagation paths","description":"Commit 6f41130c (br-43bbl fix wgpu readback polling) rewrites the
readback wait in crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193
from:

\`\`\`rust
self.device.poll(PollType::Wait { submission_index: None, timeout: Some(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT) })?;
receiver.recv_timeout(WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT)?...
\`\`\`

to a bounded poll loop:

\`\`\`rust
let deadline = Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT;
loop {
    self.device.poll(wgpu::PollType::Poll)?;
    match receiver.recv_timeout(Duration::from_millis(10)) {
        Ok(result) => { result?; break; }
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) if Instant::now() < deadline => continue,
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout) => return Err(...),
        Err(RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected) => return Err(...),
    }
}
\`\`\`

Concerns with the new implementation:

1. \`PollType::Poll\` is non-blocking by contract in wgpu 25. The loop
   relies on \`Poll\` actually driving the \`map_async\` callback forward.
   If a future wgpu point release changes \`Poll\` to be strictly
   \"dispatch-only\" (no callback invocation), the loop would always
   hit the 5s timeout. There is no test pinning this behavior.

2. The \`Disconnected\` branch is reachable only if the \`map_async\`
   closure drops without sending — that happens e.g., if wgpu drops
   the submission without invoking the callback (device lost, internal
   error). No test exercises this path.

3. \`Instant::now() + WEBGPU_READBACK_TIMEOUT\` panics on overflow
   (impossible in practice for 5s, but worth noting as a latent
   concern). Prefer \`checked_add\`.

4. Busy-wait cost: on the timeout path, the loop spins ~500 iterations
   (5000ms / 10ms) calling \`device.poll\` each time. The prior
   \`PollType::Wait\` blocked in the driver. This is a CPU-efficiency
   regression on cancellation/device-loss paths, not a correctness
   regression.

Impact: readback behavior on wgpu failure paths (device lost, long
delay, map_async disconnect) is silently unverified. A correctness
regression in \`PollType::Poll\` semantics between wgpu versions would
silently break screenshots and readback-based features.

Fix direction:
- Add a unit-level test with a mock/stub Device that controls
  \`poll()\` return timing and can force a Disconnected on the mpsc
  channel. Assert each of the four loop outcomes.
- Consider moving the readback loop into a small helper function
  that takes a \`Fn()\` poll closure + channel receiver, to make it
  unit-testable without a real GPU.
- If unit isolation is impractical, at minimum add an integration
  test that triggers a readback and asserts completion within a
  tight CPU budget (regression test for busy-wait blow-up).

Severity: medium — latent risk in a GPU-facing path that typically
works and has no automated coverage for failure modes.

Evidence: commit 6f41130c diff;
crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:168-193 at HEAD.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:25:33.419495Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062749Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:48:13.062383Z","close_reason":"Added timeout/disconnect/cancel-style readback coverage and hardened the helper deadline with checked_add","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["gpu","review","test-gap","testing","webgpu"]}

 {"id":"ft-n3mim","title":"[mor:ergonomics] Error::Runtime(String) is the '3am diagnostic dead-end' variant","description":"# Observation

Memory and grep confirm the frankenterm-core Error enum has no
\`Generic\` variant — the convention is \`Runtime(String)\` for
ad-hoc failures. Every time a caller needed a fresh error shape and
didn't want to define a typed variant, they reached for
\`Error::Runtime\`.

At 3am during an incident, the operator sees:

    Error: runtime: mux pool write failed: io error: Broken pipe (os error 32)

This is a String wrapped in an enum variant. You can't match on it,
you can't branch on it programmatically, you can't build a
dashboard row off it. The information that would make the incident
tractable — is this a transient network blip, a crashed mux, a
kernel panic, a rate limit, a disk-full? — is buried in a
string the operator has to grep for.

# Question

For each subsystem that currently stuffs into \`Error::Runtime(...)\`,
is there a typed variant that would make triage faster? Specific
candidates worth auditing:

- \`runtime_compat\` cancel / timeout wrappers — currently stringify
  the underlying failure into Runtime
- \`mux_pool\` write/read failures that aren't already covered by
  \`WeztermError\`
- \`asupersync\` lifecycle errors the caller needs to distinguish
  from transient I/O

A principle: every error the operator would act on differently
deserves a typed variant. Everything else can stay generic.

# 3am impact

This is the class of issue that doesn't show up in normal workflow.
It only bites when production is on fire. The cost is invisible
until the cost is enormous. The fix is incremental: every Runtime
creation site is a candidate for typing, and typing them one-at-a-
time pays immediately in the next incident that touches that
subsystem.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:27.030250Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57.270374Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57.270015Z","close_reason":"implemented","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["3am","ergonomics","error-types","mor"],"comments":[{"id":3600,"issue_id":"ft-n3mim","author":"jemanuel","text":"Commit 79702a50 introduces typed top-level runtime/pane/watchdog error variants plus source enums in crates/frankenterm-core/src/error.rs, deprecates Error::Runtime as a last-resort catch-all, and migrates clean operator-facing call-sites in crates/frankenterm-core/src/export.rs, crates/frankenterm-core/src/search_explain.rs, and crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs away from stringly Runtime(...). Added regression coverage for typed pane/watchdog/runtime classification and mock WezTerm error context. Local verification: rustfmt + git diff --check passed on the touched files. Requested rch lane (structured_error_ test prefix) is still in full-workspace sync on worker vmi1227854 with no compile/test result line yet. Note: crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs still has unrelated live edits in this checkout, so I intentionally did not widen this bead into that file.","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-njlk0","title":"docs: add SECURITY.md with fix history","description":"The repo has no SECURITY.md (neither at root nor under `docs/`).
Downstream users and contributors currently have no canonical
place to find:

1. the project's security posture and trust boundaries,
2. how to report a suspected vulnerability privately,
3. the audit trail of shipped security fixes.

Pane 5's 2026-04-20 → 2026-04-21 security audit shipped 7 fixes
across 8 audit passes. Beads are the current record; SECURITY.md
would give operators and auditors a single readable entry point.

## Proposed SECURITY.md scope

### § Reporting vulnerabilities
- Preferred channel (placeholder: maintainer email / GitHub private
  advisory).
- What to include in a report (repro, affected version, workspace
  config shape).
- Expected response SLO (e.g. 7d acknowledgement, 30d fix
  timeline for MEDIUM+).
- Explicit out-of-scope: DoS on unsupported platforms, findings
  that require pre-existing DB write access (captured in ft-kegvt
  / ft-ybtyg close-reasons).

### § Trust boundaries
- MCP stdio transport inherits OS uid/gid; no in-band auth.
- `.ft/` directory contents are treated as attacker-reachable
  (hostile repo clone is a real threat model).
- `ft watch` runs as the invoking user; no privilege separation.
- Pane output captured into storage is redacted on READ surfaces,
  NOT at ingest; DB file permissions are the containment boundary.

### § Fix history (session 2026-04-20 / 2026-04-21)

| Severity | Commit | Summary |
|----------|--------|---------|
| CRITICAL | 30988e53 | MCP `contract_file` / `mission_file` containment — blocks arbitrary R/W under watcher UID via hostile path args; `compute_workspace_scoped_path` with `..` reject + canonicalize + `starts_with(root_canon)`. |
| HIGH | 070117a0 | Pattern-engine ReDoS cap — `compile_rule_regex` via `RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(10M)` at all compile sites; blocks hostile `.ft/patterns/*.yaml` DoS. |
| MEDIUM | 969b9e02 | Restored-command CR/LF/ESC/C0 sanitizer in `launch_agent_{legacy,cx}`; blocks shell injection via tampered `mux_pane_state.command`. |
| MEDIUM | 598a7c13 | Session-checkpoint state_hash: replaced hardcoded `'restore'` literal with domain-separated SipHash-24 over `(session_id, pane_id_map, now_ms)`; docs realigned from misleading "BLAKE3" to accurate "SipHash-24 — not cryptographic integrity". |
| LOW | e790f481 | Diag bundle privacy scrubber — `gather_environment` routes `cwd` through `scrub_path_to_basename`; `DiagnosticOptions.include_full_cwd: false` by default; blocks `/Users/<name>/project-name` leak in shared bundles. |
| LOW | 0f38972b | `frankenterm-mux-server` SIGTERM/SIGINT handler — async-signal-safe `AtomicBool` flag polled by executor loop; ensures `wezterm_blob_leases::clear_storage()` runs on graceful shutdown instead of SIGKILL-skip. |
| LOW/MEDIUM | e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 | Size caps — `MAX_PACK_BYTES = 16 MiB` pre-parse stat check in `load_pack_from_file`; `MAX_SEND_TEXT_BYTES = 4 MiB` pre-dispatch in `WaSendTool::call`; blocks hostile-pack OOM and runaway `wa.send` payload. |

### § Regression guards
0aa93816 added victory-lap tests:
- symlink-escape rejection for MCP containment (relative + absolute variants, #[cfg(unix)]),
- ReDoS-budget headroom proof on a 100 KiB realistic input.

### § Non-normative: audit session findings classified as
defense-in-depth documentation only
- ft-yv02l (mock-code-finder catalog, closed benign)
- ft-2guux (reality-check: README cwd claim, Unix-only `ft restart`)

## Acceptance criteria
1. `/SECURITY.md` exists at repo root (the conventional GitHub
   location so it surfaces in the Security tab).
2. Sections match the four headings above.
3. Every commit hash in the Fix history table links to the
   correct git object (spot-check with `git show <hash>`).
4. Reporting channel is operator-decided — this bead should ask
   the maintainer before committing a specific contact.

## Out of scope
- Signing / provenance infrastructure (separate lane).
- Formal threat model diagram (scope creep for a docs bead).
- Translating fix-history entries into CVE identifiers (no CVE
  pipeline currently).

## Priority rationale
P2: no active exploit, no shipped user-visible gap — but the
SECURITY.md absence is a contributor-onboarding obstacle and
a GitHub "Security Policy" badge gap that every serious repo
should close.

Related:
- The seven fix commits listed above.
- 0aa93816 regression guards.
- ft-gqbpk, ft-kegvt, ft-xv561, ft-ybtyg, ft-lokam, ft-05hfm
  (all closed).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T05:34:34.794254Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T05:39:48.636983Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T05:39:48.636645Z","close_reason":"Closed by 56e7159b. +{"id":"ft-nk6u9","title":"[MEDIUM] simd_scan ansi_byte_count misses OSC/DCS/PM/APC string bodies (terminates on first letter after ESC)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

`scan_newlines_and_ansi_*` (4 variants in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/simd_scan.rs`) treats any ASCII letter (0x40..=0x7E, excluding `[`) immediately after ESC as the terminator of the escape sequence. That rule is correct for CSI (`ESC [ … letter`) and for single-char C1 controls like `ESC M` (reverse index). It is **wrong** for the five C1 introducers that open a *string* sequence:

| Intro byte | Sequence | Continues until |
|------------|----------|-----------------|
| `]` (0x5D) | OSC (Operating System Command — window titles, clipboard, hyperlinks) | ST (`ESC \\`) or BEL (0x07) |
| `P` (0x50) | DCS (Device Control String — e.g., Sixel graphics, DECRQSS replies) | ST |
| `^` (0x5E) | PM (Privacy Message) | ST |
| `_` (0x5F) | APC (Application Program Command — e.g., Kitty graphics) | ST |
| `X` (0x58) | SOS (Start Of String) | ST |

All five land in the `0x40..=0x7E && != '['` branch and prematurely exit the escape. The string body + ST/BEL bytes are then counted as **plain text**, not ANSI.

## Reproducer (currently NO test covers this)

```rust
let data = b"\x1b]0;window title\x07after";
let scan = scan_newlines_and_ansi(data);
// Current:  ansi_byte_count = 2  (just ESC + ])
// Correct:  ansi_byte_count = 19 (ESC + ] + "0;window title" + BEL)
assert_eq!(scan.ansi_byte_count, 19);
```

Same bug in the `_with_state` variants — the cross-chunk state carries only `in_escape: bool` with no notion of "string mode," so the first chunk's body bytes leak into the "non-ANSI" count.

## Blast radius

- `ansi_density` (`OutputScanMetrics::ansi_density`) underreports on panes that emit OSC/DCS/PM/APC/SOS sequences — common in production: window-title updates from shell prompts, terminfo hyperlinks, Sixel graphics, Kitty image protocol, DCS replies to DECRQSS probes.
- `ScanMetricsSummary` is consumed by compression and backpressure decision paths; an underreport makes the system think a pane is producing plain text when it is actually producing high-ANSI output.
- Not a crash, not data loss — silent wrong answer in the metrics.

## Fix plan

1. Extend `OutputScanState` with a `StringPhase` — `InString` and `InStringSawEsc` so the stateful scanner can resume a partial `ESC \\` terminator across chunk boundaries.
2. After ESC is seen, if the next byte is one of \{0x50, 0x58, 0x5D, 0x5E, 0x5F\}, transition to `InString` instead of clearing `in_escape`.
3. While in `InString`: count every byte as ANSI; terminate on BEL (0x07) or ESC \\ (0x1b 0x5c).
4. Patch all four scan functions (memchr + scalar, stateful + non-stateful).
5. Add targeted regression tests: basic OSC, OSC + ST, OSC + BEL, DCS, PM, APC, chunk split through the body, chunk split on ESC-\\, and OSC containing embedded newlines (verify newline_count is unaffected).

Scope: single file (`simd_scan.rs`). No public-API break; `OutputScanState` gains a field but `Default` still returns the "no carry" state.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:07:00.334737Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:07:26.794620Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

the existing MR3 suffix-check proves current ends with delta but
says nothing about the overlap being real.

acceptance logic (`search_window[pos..] == current[..overlap_len]`
on ingest.rs:1711) that accidentally accepts a non-matching window.

 {"id":"ft-xv561","title":"[HIGH] pattern-engine regex construction has no backtrack limit — workspace-local ReDoS via user packs","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #2 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
The pattern engine compiles user-supplied regex patterns via
`fancy_regex::Regex::new(raw)` with NO backtrack limit or match
timeout. An attacker who can drop a YAML/JSON pattern pack into
`~/.config/wa/patterns/` or a repo's `.ft/patterns/` directory can
ship a catastrophic-backtracking regex that hangs `ft watch` the
first time matching pane output triggers the pattern's anchor.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:13
  `use fancy_regex::Regex;`
- patterns.rs:512 (RuleDef::validate_regex)
  `Regex::new(regex).map_err(...)` — no `.backtrack_limit(...)`.
- patterns.rs:780 (build_engine_index)
  `let regex = Regex::new(raw).map_err(...)` — same.
- patterns.rs:920 (load_packs_from_config) + :935
  (load_pack_from_id) — pack files are loaded from
  `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` (default
  `~/.config/wa/patterns/`) AND from `.ft/patterns/` inside the
  resolved workspace root. The latter is an attacker-controlled
  directory when a repo is cloned from an untrusted source.
- mcp.rs:2010 (pattern-engine matching hot path)
  invokes `regex.captures_iter(text)` on every segment for every
  rule whose anchor matched. A single-threaded ReDoS blocks the
  matching worker; fancy_regex's default backtracking engine has
  no internal budget.

## Attack vector
1. attacker commits `.ft/patterns/evil.json` with a rule like
   ```json
   {
     "id": "evil.dos",
     "agent_type": "wezterm",
     "event_type": "noop",
     "anchors": ["a"],
     "regex": "^(a+)+$"
   }
   ```
2. user clones repo and runs `ft watch`.
3. the first pane output containing `"aaa...aaab"` (any near-match
   of the anchor) triggers captures_iter, which enters catastrophic
   backtracking. The pattern worker hangs indefinitely, wedging the
   detection pipeline.

No code execution — DoS only. But `ft watch` is the primary
operator surface; wedging the detection loop silently destroys
the "intelligent detection" claim the README makes.

## Fix direction
Two layers:

1. Compilation-time gate (quick):
   - Replace `Regex::new(raw)` with `fancy_regex::RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(1_000_000).build()`
     at patterns.rs:512 and :780. 1M steps is plenty for any
     legitimate rule and caps worst-case match cost.
   - Propagate the resulting `fancy_regex::Error::RuntimeError` from
     `captures_iter` as a `Detection` of kind `RegexRuntimeError`
     instead of hanging.

2. Match-time budget (robust):
   - Wrap each `regex.captures_iter(text)` call in a time-bounded
     harness (e.g., spawn a short-lived asupersync task with a
     100ms timeout). On timeout, quarantine the offending rule and
     surface a "rule disabled due to catastrophic match cost"
     telemetry event so operators can triage.

## Severity rationale
- HIGH (P1): attacker-controlled repo → DoS on user's machine.
- Not CRITICAL: local exploitation only (repo-clone vector), no
  network reach, no data exfil, no code execution.

## Out of scope for this bead
- RE2/Hyperscan migration (much larger rework).
- Per-rule memory budget (separate concern).

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — same audit pass
  already fixed the MCP arbitrary-file-read vector.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #2; recommend shipping the
compilation-time gate within one follow-up session.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:04:22.269417Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976852Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976108Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 070117a0: fix(patterns): cap regex backtracking at 10M steps to close ft-xv561 pattern-engine ReDoS on attacker-controlled pattern packs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","patterns","redos","security"]}
